title: mfa — developer configuration
description: MFA-specific configuration. KMS-backed secret encryption keys are configured in identity/identity's `identity.mfa_secret.*` block (see identity/config.json §mfa_secret) — this module only owns the CHALLENGE + ENROLMENT protocol tuning, not the encryption substrate.

required_for_roles.description: Which roles automatically require MFA. When a User holds any listed role, `mfa.require_challenge_complete` middleware refuses their tenant-scoped requests until an MFA challenge completes for the current session.

keys.description: Role slugs (any guard). Tenant admins can widen the list via settings (see settings.json) but cannot narrow below this default — compliance floor.

How long a newly-elevated User (freshly granted a role in `required_for_roles`) may access without enrolling MFA. 0 = enforced immediately (breaks role-promotion flows for users who need a few minutes to enrol). Compliance-adjustable via settings.

totp.description: RFC 6238 TOTP tuning. Defaults follow the RFC's most common shape — do not change these lightly, most authenticator apps hardcode the same defaults and mismatching values cause 'valid code refused' errors.

keys.description: The issuer string that appears in the authenticator app's account list (e.g. 'Stackra (alice@example.com)'). Change per-tenant via a settings override to render the tenant's brand name.
TOTP code length. 6 is the RFC 6238 default and every authenticator app supports it. Do not change.
TOTP code refresh period. 30s is the RFC 6238 default.

HMAC hash function. SHA1 is the RFC 6238 default AND the only algorithm every authenticator app supports. SHA256/SHA512 provided for spec conformance but WILL BREAK popular authenticator apps (Google Authenticator supports only SHA1).
Number of periods (before + after) the current time to accept for clock-skew tolerance. 1 = accept the previous, current, and next 30s window (90s total). Higher = more tolerance but weaker security.
Random-secret length in bytes before base32 encoding. 20 bytes = 160 bits (RFC 6238 recommended). NIST 800-63B allows 80 bits minimum but recommends 128+; 160 is standard.
How long the round-1 enrolment preview stays in Redis before eviction. 5 minutes — enough for a user to open their authenticator app, scan, wait for one full period, and type the code.
Codes per generation event. 10 balances usability (enough for reasonable churn) vs risk (too many stored plaintext by the user's credential manager becomes a liability).

Characters per code. 10 chars from a 32-character alphabet = 10 × log2(32) = 50 bits of entropy per code. Above NIST 800-63B's 20-bit minimum for a memorized secret; bcrypt-hashed at rest so brute force requires online guessing.
Crockford base32 (excludes I/L/O/U to avoid ambiguity with 1/0). 32 characters.
Insert a `-` every N chars for readability (e.g. `AB3CD-9EFGH`). Zero = no separator. Purely presentational; the separator is stripped before hashing / comparison.
When remaining_count falls below this after a MfaRecoveryCodeConsumed, fire MfaBackupCodeCountLow (which dispatches the low-recovery-codes notification). Tenant admins can raise this via settings.
Master toggle. When false, WebAuthn endpoints refuse with 503 + WebauthnCredential registration is refused at the observer level.
Human-readable relying-party name shown in the browser's authenticator UI. Tenant admins can override via settings to display their brand.
Relying-party identifier. Null = derive from the request's Host header (recommended for multi-tenant deployments). Explicitly set only when running behind a reverse proxy that mangles Host.

Require the authenticator to create a resident key (usable without a username prompt — a.k.a. 'passkey'). Off by default because roaming keys (like classic YubiKeys) are non-resident. Set true for a pure-passkey tenant environment.

Whether the authenticator MUST verify the user (PIN, biometric, etc.). `required` = MFA becomes single-step (WebAuthn subsumes the password); `preferred` = ask if the authenticator supports it, accept if not; `discouraged` = classic one-tap. `preferred` is the safe default per W3C guidance.

Attestation preference. `none` = don't collect attestation (privacy-preserving; the default per W3C). `direct` = collect and verify (needed for compliance regimes that mandate attestation-based auditor lists like FIDO Alliance's Metadata Service).
Browser-side timeout for the WebAuthn ceremony (register + assert). 60 seconds is standard; lower confuses users on slow biometric readers, higher is a poor UX.

Cap on WebauthnCredential rows per Identity. 10 covers 'phone + laptop + backup hardware key + spare' comfortably. Hard cap — refuses registration with `WEBAUTHN_MAX_CREDENTIALS_REACHED` (422) at the observer level.
How long a disabled credential is retained before CleanupDisabledWebauthnCredentialsJob hard-deletes it. Retention supports post-incident audit — 'was credential X active at time T'.

challenge.description: Challenge-ledger tuning. The auth_mfa_challenges table is OWNED BY identity/auth; these keys shape the behaviour when mfa dispatches into it.

keys.description: How long a challenge row is valid for verification. 5 minutes matches identity/auth's default; changing here should be coordinated with the sibling module.
How many verify attempts a challenge row allows before invalidation. On third failure the challenge row is destroyed + the caller must restart from login.
After max_attempts exhausted, the caller cannot issue a fresh challenge for N minutes. Rate-limits attackers who cycle through challenges. Applies per-Identity.

step_up.description: Step-up authentication policy — the `mfa.require_recent_challenge` middleware reads these keys.

keys.description: How long after a successful MFA challenge a sensitive-write action (password change, MFA disable, recovery-code regen, webauthn-credential delete) is allowed without a fresh challenge. Short window balances UX (don't force N challenges during one admin session) vs security (stolen sessions age out fast).
Action IDs (from #[UsesTrait(HasMfa)]) that participate in the step-up gate. Enumerated here so the enforcement is declarative + auditable.
Rolling window over which failure counts + distinct-IPs are aggregated.
N failures within the window trips a signal.
Signal fires ONLY when the failures span N or more distinct IPs — reduces false positives from a legitimate user retyping.

Once a suspicious-activity notification is dispatched, further suppress duplicate dispatches for N hours to avoid spam during an active attack. The underlying signal keeps firing (metrics + audit); only the user-facing notification is debounced.
When true, every mutating Artisan command (force-disable, regenerate-recovery-codes) must be invoked with --reason=<text> (audit-logged). Off in dev; on in staging + prod.
